metadata.description: Banks and mortgage lenders, with how each makes money from housing.

properties.name: Royal Bank of Canada (RBC)
properties.how_they_make_money: 5-year fixed mortgages with mandatory renewal. Each renewal resets the amortization clock — frontloaded interest restarts. Also finances developers' construction loans on the same projects whose units they later mortgage to buyers.
properties.notable: Big 6 Canadian bank. CMHC-insured mortgages transfer default risk to the federal government while RBC keeps the interest.

properties.name: Toronto-Dominion Bank (TD)
properties.how_they_make_money: Same renewal-reset model as RBC. Heavy in HELOCs (home equity lines of credit) — turns appreciated housing into a revolving credit product the homeowner pays interest on indefinitely.
properties.notable: Big 6.

properties.name: Scotiabank
properties.how_they_make_money: STEP product bundles mortgage + HELOC + credit cards under one collateral envelope — pulls more household debt under the housing-collateral umbrella, increasing total interest capture per customer.
properties.notable: Big 6.

properties.name: Bank of Montreal (BMO)
properties.how_they_make_money: Standard renewal-reset. Major in commercial real estate lending to REITs and institutional landlords.
properties.notable: Big 6.

properties.name: CIBC
properties.how_they_make_money: Standard renewal-reset. Heavy uninsured mortgage exposure in Toronto/Vancouver — higher rates, higher margins, but balance-sheet risk if prices fall.
properties.notable: Big 6.

properties.name: National Bank of Canada
properties.how_they_make_money: Quebec-dominant; renewal-reset model.
properties.notable: Smallest of the Big 6.

properties.name: JPMorgan Chase
properties.how_they_make_money: Originates 30-yr fixed mortgages, sells most to Fannie/Freddie, keeps the servicing fee (≈0.25% of balance annually for the life of the loan). Plus trades the MBS the loans become.
properties.notable: Originate-to-distribute. Loan risk leaves the balance sheet; servicing income stays.

properties.name: Wells Fargo
properties.how_they_make_money: Largest mortgage servicer in US for years; servicing fees on trillions in unpaid principal balance. Origination volume cyclical, servicing revenue steady.
properties.notable: Servicing is the annuity; origination is the funnel that feeds it.

properties.name: Bank of America
properties.how_they_make_money: Same originate-to-distribute model. Acquired Countrywide in 2008 — inherited both the servicing portfolio and the subprime litigation tail.
properties.notable: Cautionary tale: the Countrywide acquisition shows that servicing rights come with liability when underwriting was bad.

properties.name: Rocket Mortgage
properties.how_they_make_money: Pure originator. Closes the loan, immediately sells it (gain-on-sale revenue), often sells servicing rights too. No deposit base — funded via warehouse lines. Volume game.
properties.notable: Largest US originator. Demonstrates that origination and servicing are now distinct businesses owned by different firms.

properties.name: Lloyds Banking Group
properties.how_they_make_money: Largest UK mortgage lender. Short fix terms (2/5 yr) with renewal-reset capture, like Canada. Owns Halifax — second mortgage brand under same group.
properties.notable: UK uses short fixes — renewals are the bank-favorable mechanism.

properties.name: Barclays
properties.how_they_make_money: UK retail mortgages plus investment bank arm packages and trades MBS for institutional clients.
properties.notable: Has both a consumer mortgage book and an MBS trading desk under one roof.

properties.name: HSBC
properties.how_they_make_money: Cross-border mortgage product — Hong Kong residents buying London, mainland Chinese buying Vancouver/Toronto/Sydney. HSBC arbitrages currency, jurisdiction, and rate differentials.
properties.notable: The vehicle through which Asian capital enters Anglosphere housing markets.

properties.name: Nationwide Building Society
properties.how_they_make_money: Member-owned mutual — surplus returned to members (savers + borrowers) rather than shareholders. Same mortgage product, different ownership extraction model.
properties.notable: Proof that the mortgage business model is not inherently extractive — corporate structure determines where the surplus goes.

properties.name: Commonwealth Bank of Australia (CBA)
properties.how_they_make_money: Variable-rate mortgages — bank passes RBA rate moves to borrowers immediately on increases, slowly on decreases (the 'mortgage cliff' asymmetry). 60%+ of Australian mortgages are variable.
properties.notable: Biggest of the Big 4. Australian housing/bank concentration is among highest globally.

properties.name: Westpac
properties.how_they_make_money: Variable-rate, interest-only mortgages historically common — investor-friendly, generates pure interest revenue with no principal paydown.
properties.notable: Big 4.

properties.name: ANZ
properties.how_they_make_money: Variable-rate mortgages. Also significant NZ mortgage exposure.
properties.notable: Big 4.

properties.name: National Australia Bank (NAB)
properties.how_they_make_money: Commercial real estate strength — finances apartment developers, then mortgages the units to buyers.
properties.notable: Big 4. Same vertical capture pattern as Canadian Big 6.

properties.name: HDFC Bank
properties.how_they_make_money: Merged with HDFC Ltd in 2023 — combined the largest home loan specialist with the largest private bank. Rates 8-9%. On a 20-yr loan, interest paid ≈ 1.8x principal.
properties.notable: Merger consolidated India's mortgage market dramatically.

properties.name: State Bank of India (SBI)
properties.how_they_make_money: State-owned. Cheapest rates (slim margin to ~7.5%), but volume is enormous — largest mortgage lender in India by book.
properties.notable: State ownership lowers headline rates but doesn't change the lifetime-interest-extraction model.

properties.name: ICICI Bank
properties.how_they_make_money: Floating-rate home loans — reset with RBI repo rate. Borrowers absorb interest-rate risk.
properties.notable: Private sector leader before HDFC merger.

properties.name: LIC Housing Finance
properties.how_they_make_money: Subsidiary of state insurer LIC — uses insurance float as cheap funding for mortgages. Insurance + lending vertical capture.
properties.notable: Insurance-funded lending — depositors' premiums effectively finance other depositors' mortgages.

properties.name: Bajaj Housing Finance
properties.how_they_make_money: Fast-growing NBFC. Some securitization (PTC pools) — early-stage version of US-style MBS market.
properties.notable: India is on the early curve of mortgage securitization. Track this — it's how lifetime extraction multiplies.

properties.name: Deutsche Bank
properties.how_they_make_money: Long-fixed (10-15 yr) mortgages — Pfandbrief covered bonds fund the loans. Lower lifetime interest extraction than US/Canada model because rates are fixed for long periods and renewal-reset isn't standard.
properties.notable: German mortgage market is structurally less extractive.

properties.name: KfW (Kreditanstalt für Wiederaufbau)
properties.how_they_make_money: State development bank. Subsidised loans for energy-efficient renovation and first-time buyers. Operates at near-zero margin by mandate.
properties.notable: Counter-example: a major housing lender whose business model is not interest extraction. Worth studying as a template.

properties.name: Mitsubishi UFJ Financial Group
properties.how_they_make_money: Japanese mortgage rates ~1.5% — slim per-loan margin. Bank revenue comes from volume + bundled services (insurance, brokerage).
properties.notable: Lowest-rate developed market. Slim margins, but also slim wealth transfer from occupiers.

properties.name: Japan Housing Finance Agency (JHF)
properties.how_they_make_money: State-backed long-fixed (Flat 35) mortgages, securitized via JHF MBS. Government effectively underwrites the rate stability.
properties.notable: Japan's equivalent of Fannie/Freddie.

properties.name: HSBC Hong Kong
properties.how_they_make_money: Dominant HK mortgage lender. HIBOR-linked variable rates. Cross-border lending into London (see HSBC London entry).
properties.notable: Same parent group as HSBC London — fully vertically integrated cross-border mortgage operation.

properties.name: Bank of China (Hong Kong)
properties.how_they_make_money: Channels mainland Chinese borrower demand into HK property. State ownership = political utility lever.
properties.notable: Mortgage lending as instrument of capital channeling, not just profit.

properties.name: DBS Bank
properties.how_they_make_money: Largest SG mortgage lender. SORA-linked floating rates after recent reform. HDB (public housing) loans go through HDB itself, not banks — so DBS competes mainly for private condo + executive condo segment.
properties.notable: Singapore's public-private housing split limits bank capture on ~80% of housing stock.

properties.name: OCBC
properties.how_they_make_money: Same private-segment focus as DBS.

properties.name: ING
properties.how_they_make_money: Long-fixed Dutch mortgages, often 20-30 yr fixed — like German model. NHG (national mortgage guarantee) transfers default risk to the state.

properties.name: Rabobank
properties.how_they_make_money: Cooperative — like Nationwide UK. Member-owned. Largest NL mortgage lender by some measures.
properties.notable: Another non-shareholder ownership structure providing the same product.
